title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Sam has just moved to a new city and is not sure how to get around. Write an email to Sam giving advice on the best ways to travel around the city, explaining the advantages of different transport options, and warning them about any common problems to watch out for.

Write your email to Sam.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Sam,

How exciting that you have finally moved! Getting around a new city can seem daunting at first, but I promise you will get the hang of it quickly. Here is my honest advice.

The underground is almost certainly going to be your best friend for longer journeys — it is fast, reliable, and far cheaper than taxis. Make sure you download the official transport app before you leave the house, as it shows real-time departures and route changes.

For shorter trips, I would highly recommend the city bike scheme. It is great exercise, affordable, and you avoid the stress of traffic entirely. Just remember to wear a helmet and be careful at busy junctions.

One thing to watch out for: buses can be extremely unreliable during rush hour, so always allow extra time if you have somewhere important to be.

Finally, avoid unlicensed taxis at night — they can be significantly more expensive and less safe than registered services.

Hope this helps! Let me know when you are settled.

Best,
Jordan

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You have been asked to write a review for an English-language travel website. Write a review of a transport app, bike-sharing scheme, or public transport system that you have used. Describe what it offers, evaluate how useful and reliable it is, and recommend whether other people should use it.

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
CityBike Share Scheme — A Commuter's Best Friend?

Having used the CityBike share scheme daily for the past six months, I feel well-placed to offer an honest assessment of what is rapidly becoming one of the most popular ways to get around our city.

The scheme operates through a straightforward app, allowing users to locate and unlock bikes within seconds. The pricing is genuinely competitive — a monthly subscription costs less than a weekly bus pass, making it excellent value for regular commuters.

In terms of reliability, I have rarely been unable to find a bike near my home or office. The bikes themselves are sturdy and well-maintained, and the GPS navigation feature built into the handlebars is a surprisingly useful touch.

However, the scheme does have weaknesses. Docking stations in residential areas are often full during peak hours, meaning you can be left carrying your bag and searching for an alternative parking spot.

Overall, I would enthusiastically recommend CityBike to anyone living within the city centre. It is affordable, environmentally friendly, and genuinely transforms the daily commute.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a newspaper article claiming that cycling is too dangerous to be encouraged in cities. Write a letter to the newspaper editor disagreeing with this view, explaining why better cycling infrastructure would benefit the city, and suggesting specific improvements the local authority should make.

Write your letter to the editor.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I read your recent article on urban cycling with great interest, though I must respectfully but firmly disagree with its central argument. Cycling is not inherently dangerous — it becomes dangerous only when cities fail to provide the infrastructure necessary to make it safe.

The solution is not to discourage cycling but to invest properly in it. Cities across northern Europe have demonstrated conclusively that when protected cycle lanes, clear road markings, and adequate bike storage are provided, cycling rates increase dramatically while accident numbers fall.

The benefits of expanding cycling infrastructure extend well beyond individual health. Fewer cars on the road means reduced congestion, cleaner air, and lower carbon emissions — outcomes that benefit every resident, not just cyclists.

I would urge the local authority to commission a proper cycling infrastructure review, consult with cycling organisations, and commit to a ten-year investment plan with measurable targets.

Criticising cyclists for using dangerous roads misses the point entirely. The roads need to change.

Yours sincerely,
A Regular Cyclist
